project__agency_information.lead_agency.answer: City of Riverbend Community Development Department

project_location.cross_streets.answer: East Riverbend Boulevard and Willow Creek Road

project_location.within_2_miles_schools.answer: Riverbend High School; Willow Creek Elementary School; Feather River Community College satellite campus

development_type.transportation_type.answer: On-site public streets, roundabout, Class II and Class I bicycle facilities, and enhanced transit stops along East Riverbend Boulevard

development_type.other_development_type.answer: Neighborhood park and greenway system (approximately 6.0 acres), community center (8,500 sq ft), and stormwater quality basins integrated as open space amenities

project_issues_discussed_in_document.oth.answer: Energy consumption and conservation; greenhouse gas emissions and climate change resiliency; environmental justice and equitable access to parks and transit; construction-period health risk assessment for nearby sensitive receptors.

land_use__project_description.present_la.answer:
The approximately 48.6-acre project site is currently a mix of fallow agricultural land and disturbed ruderal grassland historically used for seasonal hay production and limited grazing. There are no existing structures on the site aside from an abandoned wooden equipment shed and remnants of old barbed-wire fencing along a portion of the southern boundary. Surrounding uses include a single-family residential subdivision to the west, the Feather River and associated riparian corridor to the north, and a mix of low-density residential and neighborhood commercial uses to the south and east.

Under the City of Riverbend General Plan (2035), the site is designated primarily as Mixed Use Neighborhood (MU-N) with a small portion along East Riverbend Boulevard designated as Neighborhood Commercial (NC). The existing zoning is split between Agricultural-Urban Reserve (A-UR) and Interim Residential (R-1-I), reflecting the property’s historic agricultural use and its location within the City’s sphere of influence and planned growth area. The project includes a request to rezone the entire site to a Planned Development (PD) district consistent with the proposed Riverbend Mixed-Use Village Specific Plan, with subdistricts for Residential Neighborhood (PD-R), Mixed-Use Main Street (PD-MU), and Parks/Open Space (PD-OS).

land_use__project_description.project_de.answer:
The Riverbend Mixed-Use Village Project is a master-planned, infill-style development intended to create a walkable, transit-supportive neighborhood that provides a mix of housing types, neighborhood-serving commercial uses, office employment opportunities, and public open space amenities within the City of Riverbend. The project is proposed by Riverbend Village Partners, LLC in coordination with the City of Riverbend Community Development Department.

Overall Program and Land Use Components
The project would develop approximately 48.6 acres into a compact, mixed-use village organized around a central "Main Street" corridor. Key components include:

1. Residential Development (approximately 24.3 acres; 228 units total)
- 92 single-family detached homes on individual lots (average lot size approximately 4,000 square feet), primarily located on the western and southern portions of the site. These units are envisioned for a mix of young families and move-down buyers, with a variety of one- and two-story floor plans.
- 74 townhome units in attached 3- to 4-plex buildings along and near the Main Street corridor, providing more affordable ownership options and a transition between the commercial core and single-family neighborhood.
- 62 multi-family apartment units in two 3-story buildings above ground-floor neighborhood-serving commercial, targeted toward young professionals, seniors, and small households. These units would include a mix of studios, one-bedroom, and two-bedroom units.
- A minimum of 15% of the total units (approximately 34 units) are proposed to be deed-restricted as affordable to low- and moderate-income households through an inclusionary housing agreement with the City. The affordable units would be distributed among the townhome and apartment components to avoid concentration in any single building or block.

2. Commercial and Office Development (approximately 9.7 acres total)
- Approximately 65,000 square feet of neighborhood-serving commercial uses concentrated along the Main Street corridor and at the intersection of East Riverbend Boulevard and Willow Creek Road. Anticipated uses include a small-format grocery store (15,000–20,000 square feet), cafes and restaurants with outdoor seating, a neighborhood pharmacy, personal services (e.g., hair salon, dry cleaner), and small specialty retail shops.
- Approximately 40,000 square feet of office space located in two- and three-story buildings. Potential tenants include medical and dental offices, professional services (e.g., accountants, architects, real estate), and small co-working spaces targeting remote workers who currently commute out of Riverbend. The office component is expected to provide approximately 165 on-site employment opportunities.
- Ground-floor commercial spaces along Main Street would be designed with flexible storefronts, generous sidewalks, shade trees, and street furniture to encourage pedestrian activity and outdoor dining.

3. Parks, Open Space, and Community Amenities (approximately 6.0 acres)
- A 3.5-acre centrally located neighborhood park with a multi-purpose turf field, children’s play equipment with shade structures, picnic areas with barbecues, a half-court basketball area, and informal seating areas. The park would be publicly accessible and designed to serve both project residents and the surrounding community.
- A 1.0-acre linear greenway and Class I multi-use path along the northern edge of the site, adjacent to the Willow Creek drainage, providing a landscaped buffer, habitat enhancement plantings, and a separated pedestrian/bicycle connection to East Riverbend Boulevard and the broader city trail network.
- Approximately 1.5 acres of distributed open space, including pocket parks, landscaped paseos between residential blocks, and stormwater quality basins designed as attractive, usable open space with native and drought-tolerant plantings.
- An 8,500-square-foot single-story community center located near the neighborhood park, which would include a multi-purpose meeting room, small kitchenette, flexible classroom space for after-school programs and community workshops, and office space for a homeowners association and community services. The community center is intended to host events such as neighborhood association meetings, senior exercise classes, and seasonal farmers’ markets.

4. Circulation, Access, and Parking
- Primary vehicular access would be provided from East Riverbend Boulevard via a new signalized intersection aligned with Willow Creek Road, including dedicated left-turn lanes and enhanced crosswalks. A secondary right-in/right-out access would be provided approximately 600 feet east of the main entrance to disperse traffic.
- The internal street network would consist of a modified grid pattern with short blocks, on-street parking, and traffic-calming features such as bulb-outs and raised intersections at key pedestrian crossings. A small single-lane roundabout is proposed at the intersection of Main Street and Park Way to slow traffic and enhance safety.
- Bicycle facilities would include Class II bike lanes along East Riverbend Boulevard and Main Street, as well as a Class I multi-use path along the northern greenway connecting to the citywide trail system. Secure bicycle parking racks would be provided near all commercial buildings, the community center, and the neighborhood park.
- Transit enhancements include the construction of two new bus turnouts and sheltered transit stops along East Riverbend Boulevard, with seating, lighting, and real-time arrival information (subject to coordination with the regional transit agency). The project is intended to support increased transit service frequency over time by concentrating housing and employment along the corridor.
- Parking would be provided through a combination of private garages for single-family and townhome units, surface parking lots behind or beside commercial and office buildings, and limited on-street parking. Shared parking strategies and time-of-day management are anticipated for the mixed-use core to reduce overall parking demand and encourage walking.

5. Infrastructure, Utilities, and Stormwater Management
- The project would extend existing municipal water, sewer, and recycled water lines from East Riverbend Boulevard into the site. All utilities would be installed underground to the extent feasible, including electrical, telecommunications, and natural gas.
- Stormwater runoff would be managed through a combination of low-impact development (LID) features such as bioretention planters, vegetated swales, and permeable pavement in selected parking areas, as well as larger, landscaped detention and water quality basins located within the greenway and open space network. These facilities are designed to meet or exceed the City’s stormwater quality requirements and to mimic pre-development hydrologic conditions to the extent practicable.
- The project does not include a new wastewater treatment plant or drinking water treatment facility; instead, it would connect to existing regional facilities. Capacity analysis has been conducted as part of the environmental review to ensure that the increased demand can be accommodated with planned system improvements.

6. Phasing and Construction
- Construction is anticipated to occur in three primary phases over approximately 6–8 years, depending on market conditions and absorption rates.
  • Phase 1 would include backbone infrastructure (roads and utilities), the central neighborhood park, the community center, and approximately one-third of the residential units, along with a portion of the commercial space at the East Riverbend Boulevard/Willow Creek Road intersection.
  • Phase 2 would build out the Main Street mixed-use core, including the majority of the commercial and office space and the apartment buildings, as well as additional townhome units and completion of the greenway trail connection.
  • Phase 3 would complete the remaining single-family neighborhoods, pocket parks, and any remaining commercial pads. Each phase would be required to provide proportional infrastructure and open space improvements to ensure that early residents have access to key amenities.
- Construction activities would generally occur between 7:00 a.m. and 6:00 p.m. on weekdays, with limited weekend work as needed. Standard dust control measures (e.g., water trucks, covering haul loads, stabilized construction entrances) and noise-reducing practices (e.g., mufflers on equipment, locating stationary equipment away from existing homes) would be implemented.

7. Objectives and Community Context
The project is designed to advance several adopted City policy objectives, including:
- Providing a mix of housing types and affordability levels to address identified regional housing needs, particularly for workforce households and seniors wishing to age in place.
- Supporting a more balanced jobs–housing ratio in Riverbend by creating local employment opportunities within walking and biking distance of new and existing neighborhoods.
- Encouraging alternative modes of transportation (walking, biking, and transit) through compact, mixed-use design, an interconnected street network, and high-quality pedestrian and bicycle infrastructure.
- Enhancing access to parks and open space by creating a new neighborhood park, greenway, and trail connections that are accessible not only to project residents but also to the broader community.
- Implementing sustainable design features such as water-efficient landscaping, LID stormwater management, and building designs that are solar-ready and meet or exceed current energy efficiency standards.

From a personal perspective as the lead agency contact, I (Melissa J. Ortega) have been closely involved in neighborhood outreach for this project over the past two years. We have held multiple community workshops where nearby residents expressed concerns about traffic, parking overflow, and potential loss of informal open space where some neighbors currently walk their dogs. In response, the project applicant agreed to incorporate additional visitor parking on key residential streets, widen sidewalks on Main Street and near the park, and preserve a continuous greenway along the northern edge to maintain a sense of openness and visual connection to the Feather River corridor. Several long-time residents also asked for assurances that the project would include family-friendly amenities and safe routes to school; accordingly, the site plan now shows enhanced crosswalks and traffic-calming near the primary pedestrian paths leading toward Willow Creek Elementary and Riverbend High School.

Overall, the Riverbend Mixed-Use Village Project is intended to transform an underutilized edge-of-town agricultural parcel into a complete neighborhood that integrates housing, jobs, services, and recreation, while respecting the adjacent river corridor and existing residential areas. The accompanying Mitigated Negative Declaration evaluates the potential environmental impacts associated with this buildout and identifies mitigation measures to reduce impacts to less-than-significant levels wherever feasible.
